EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability ahead of an arctic front resulted in strong snow showers with gusty winds during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers, wind gusts to 40 miles per hour, and a sharp temperature drop along an advancing arctic front resulted in severe driving conditions on Interstate 90 from near Drummond to Butte. Similar road conditions were observed on US Highway 12 from MacDonald Pass to Elliston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold trough moved into the state for more sub-zero temperatures and wind chills. Warning criteria wind chill values (-40F) were more common with this surge than the previous one as a 1044 mb surface high moved southward from the Canadian Prairies. The cold air was followed by strong westerly flow, which brought another round of strong surface winds to the plains and portions of southwest MT. Temperatures remained below freezing along the Rocky for a portion of the wind event and brought another round of ground blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Sweetgrass station recorded a -45F wind chill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow aloft became more established over the Northern Rockies as 50 kt + H700 winds were brought to the surface by diurnal mixing and surface pressure gradients.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Sunburst station measured a 56 kt (64 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow aloft became more established over the Northern Rockies as 50 kt + H700 winds were brought to the surface by diurnal mixing and surface pressure gradients.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at Kevin measured a 52 kt (60 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow aloft became more established over the Northern Rockies as 50 kt + H700 winds were brought to the surface by diurnal mixing and surface pressure gradients.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow aloft gradually became stronger and more westerly ahead of an approaching Pacific trough. The combination of this and a lee-side trough and its attendant cold front, resulted in stronger wind gusts moving off the Rocky Mountain Front onto the plains and into portions of southwestern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Oilmont MDA (8 ENE Ferdig) measured a 51 kt (59 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a broad upper level trough set up shop across the area, cold air pushed into the area from Canada. This brought subzero temperatures to most of the area, with some areas getting into the teens below zero. Winds along the Rocky Mountain Front were just enough to result in a few isolated areas of wind chills to -40F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two mesonet sites in the Browning/Two Medicine Bridge area reported wind chills of -40F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level ridge flattened out across the region, upper level flow increased across the area. This upper level flow downsloped along the Rocky Mountain Front and raced across the plains, resulting in wind gusts over 60 mph across portions of the area, particularly along the Rocky Mountain Front and along the northern slopes of the Little Belt and Snowy Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station 4 miles east of Cascade (mtm76) reported wind gusts between 58-62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian cold front and overrunning moist southwesterly flow in the mid levels resulted in mainly light snow across the region. Two reports of greater than 6 inches of snow were observed in favored upslope location across eastern portions of North-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter 1 ene of East Helena reports 8 inches new snow overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across Central and Southwest Montana, leading to strong, gusty winds, small hail, brief downpours, and an array of lightning. Dry and seasonally mild conditions returned thereafter, leading to an increase of westerly winds in excess 55 mph across the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains. Cooler temperatures returned during the latter half of the week, with temperatures 10 to 20 degrees below average and widespread precipitation. Lower elevations saw generally a tenth to half an inch of liquid precipitation, whereas higher elevations saw several inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Ginger Raws recorded a wind gust of 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow produced slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, along with blowing and drifting snow, caused very tough driving conditions across the area. Total snowfall amounts include: 22 inches Bull Lake, 12 to 18 inches Libby and Eureka, 7 to 10 inches in both Yaak and Trout Creek. Snow bands brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to US-93 between Olney and Whitefish during the evening of Dec. 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of gusty winds and cold arctic air created dangerously cold wind chills in Sheridan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site (MTCOM), temperatures in the teens below zero and breezy winds created intermittent periods of wind chills of -40 or worse during the night of January 8th. The peak wind chill of -41 was recorded at 10:56 PM on the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The week-long arctic cold spell from the end of December 2021 spilled over into the first day of 2022, with continued well below zero temperatures and pockets of light winds, which created dangerously cold wind chill values for many northeast Montana locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beach, ND ASOS site (K20U) near Wibaux County, temperatures in the teens below zero and breezy winds caused a wind chill of -40 to be recorded at 2:55 AM on January 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The week-long arctic cold spell from the end of December 2021 spilled over into the first day of 2022, with continued well below zero temperatures and pockets of light winds, which created dangerously cold wind chill values for many northeast Montana locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple sites across Richland County, temperatures in the teens and 20s below zero and breezy winds created intermittent periods of wind chills of -40 or worse during the early morning of January 1st. The peak wind chill of -46 was recorded at 7:56 AM at the Sidney-Richland Airport ASOS Site (KSDY).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough and surface low pressure area interacted with abundant moisture to bring significant mountain snowfall, generally above 5000 feet. Backcountry recreation was the primary impact with hunting season ongoing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Malta W ADA mesonet site 5 miles west of Malta at 11:45 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough and surface low pressure area interacted with abundant moisture to bring significant mountain snowfall, generally above 5000 feet. Backcountry recreation was the primary impact with hunting season ongoing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts were highly variable but SNOTELs above 6000 feet reported 8-16 inches of new snow. Philipsburg reported 12 inches of snow as well. Interstate 90 at Homestake Pass was closed for 7 hours due to icy conditions and semi-truck slide offs on the evening of the 23rd. Chains were also required for a time on MacDonald Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls cause severe driving conditions during the evening commute near Missoula and also near Garrison.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL Dual pol radar confirmed similar heavy snowfall rates were possible with the band over Interstate 90. Severe driving conditions were hoisted by the MDT on Highway 12 between Garrison and Avon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain led to severe driving conditions and school closures from Troy to Libby to Marion on US-2. Periods of moderate to heavy snowfall occurred with amounts ranging from 9 inches in Marion, 10 inches Bull Lake, over a foot of snow between Troy and Libby and a whopping 18.5 inches at Troy. Gusty winds between Dickey Lake and Fortine caused drifting snow on US-93. One spotter located in higher terrain above and south of Rexford reported a total snowfall amount of 24 inches.

The peak wind chill of -49 was recorded from 1:29 AM to 1:39 AM on the 6th at the US-2 Stateline DOT site (MTSTA).

The peak wind chill of -45 was recorded at 3:11 AM on the 5th at the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL).

The peak wind chill of -46 was recorded at 5:35 AM and 6:35 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough and surface low pressure area interacted with abundant moisture to bring significant mountain snowfall, generally above 5000 feet. Backcountry recreation was the primary impact with hunting season ongoing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 12 inches of snow was reported above 6000 feet with significant impacts to backcountry users.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate amounts of snow fell within two days and brought 8 to 12 inches of snow from West Glacier to Marias Pass. Montana Department of Transportation hoisted severe driving conditions on US-2 from 5 miles west of Marias Pass to Browning due to blowing and drifting snow from strong southwest winds gusting to 36 mph on the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic weather system brought 4 to 8 inches of snow from the Flathead Valley to Polson and 2 to 4 inches of snow in the Mission Valley. This resulted in snow-covered roads and slick travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow fell early on the 6th which resulted in multiple school closures from St. Regis to Alberton. Heavy snow kept falling in the mountains but changed to freezing rain in the valleys by daybreak on the 7th. Severe driving conditions were reported on Highway 200. Power outages were reported between Thompson Falls and Trout Creek. Snow amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches in the valleys, 12 inches Haugan, 16 inches Heron and over 2 feet in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple avalanches occurred on Interstate 90 with full blockage of the driving lane on the westbound side from mile-markers 7.5 to 10, situated west of Saltese. Estimating from the MDT photo, the largest slide was 5 feet tall and between 100 to 300 feet wide. The westbound lanes were closed the following night due to further avalanche risk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow, amounting between 5 and 10 inches fell initially before changing to freezing rain. Roads became very icy which resulted in multiple vehicle crashes, school closures and delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 2 feet of heavy, wet snow fell at both Lolo Pass, and south of Conner to Lost Trail Pass, and caused hazardous road conditions. Heavy snowfall increased the avalanche risk which resulted in the closure of US-12 west of Lolo Pass for a total of 3 days. Severe driving conditions were reported on US-12 between Lolo and Lolo Pass for 3 hours on the morning of the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the first part of the storm, 9 to 15 inches of heavy snow fell, and disrupted travel. The snow changed to freezing rain by the 7th which created icy, and severe driving conditions. Facebook reports mentioned that a multi-vehicle pile-up stopped traffic near Clinton on Interstate 90 during the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 200 was blocked by an avalanche just east of Bonner at mile marker 3. The dimensions were approximately 100 feet wide by 5 feet high.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic intrusion coincided with a moist Pacific wave associated with an atmospheric river, and brought high impacts including heavy snow to both valleys and mountains, hazardous travel, avalanches, tree damage, road closures, power outages and icy roads from freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow fell, ranging from near 8 inches at both Philipsburg and Butte, to 14 inches at both 7 miles west of Anaconda, and in Ovando. Severe driving conditions were hoisted near daybreak on the 5th between Anaconda and Warm Springs. Interstate 90 was closed for a period between Butte and Whitehall on the morning of the 6th due to several tractor trailers that had slid off the road. Road conditions were very slick between Garrison and MacDonald Pass where a plow slid off the roadway during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred along the Interstate 90 corridor in west-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several intense rain showers moved over Interstate 90 between Alberton and Superior,  and caused ice accretion and slick conditions. The eastbound lane was closed for several hours due to an overturned truck and two jackknifed tractor trailers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred along the Interstate 90 corridor in west-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers brought around a tenth of an inch of ice accretion to the Missoula area which caused several vehicle crashes, an ambulance to slide off the road and a vehicle with a trailer to get jackknifed on I-90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred along the Interstate 90 corridor in west-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers caused I-90 to become slick and caused two jackknifes and a slide-off between Clinton and Bearmouth. Black ice was also reported between Bonner and Potomac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific jet increased westerly flow across the Northern Rockies. The combination of strong surface high pressure across the Rockies and an emerging surface trough east of the Rockies, in addition to the Pacific Jet, resulted in strong winds, primarily across the western Hi-Line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station srym8 Saint Mary RAWS measured a 64 mph wind gust. Additionally, the Logan Pass National Park mesonet measured a 59 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly winds in the mid levels (H700) were approximately 2 standard deviations above normal for the time of year. Through mountain waves, these stronger winds were mixed down to the surface across North-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ambient Weather Station LJ Johnson Farms measured a lone gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire initiated in the Beaverhead-Deerlodge National Forest in late August and persisted into late October. This fire affected both NWS Great Falls and NWS Missoula's areas of responsibility and will also be noted in September and October Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trail Ridge Fire began in late August across extreme northwest Beaverhead County, approximately 22 miles northwest of Wisdom, near the Beaverhead/Ravalli County line. 18,138 acres burned across the two counties with the cause of the fire being officially listed as lightning. The fire persisted from late August, through September, and into late October and will also be listed in Storm Data for those respective months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching frontal system from the west and a weak disturbance riding down the Canadian Rockies was just strong enough to bring high winds from aloft down to the surface, especially downstream of higher elevation locations, such as southwest Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speeds of at least 40 mph were common in the Little Rocky Mountains from early afternoon to the nighttime hours. The peak wind gusts of 58 mph were recorded between 7:14 PM and 7:15 PM at the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere set up a strong low-level jet stream over our region, which easily reached down to the surface, especially just downwind of our higher elevation areas in southwest Phillips County.  A subtle embedded low pressure disturbance kept strong and gusty winds sustained through most of the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent wind speeds of at least 40 mph were common in the Little Rocky Mountains from early afternoon to the early nighttime hours. The peak wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at 2:43 PM at the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, three snowmobilers and two snow bikers were in Sheep Creek on the south face of Miller Mountain north of Cooke City. The two snow bikers were high on the slope when one stopped on a small ridge while the other climbed higher and triggered a large avalanche. The avalanche carried him down through a gully and over a cliff where he was partially buried near the toe of the debris. His arm and airbag were visible and his head was 1 foot under the surface. Resuscitation efforts were unsuccessful. The avalanche was 800 feet wide, averaged 2 feet deep (estimated) and ran 1200 vertical feet. The avalanche likely broke on facets and depth hoar at the bottom of the snowpack in an area that was thinly covered and recently wind-loaded. The debris was up to 15 feet deep. For a full, detailed report, please see www.mtavalanche.com.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Episode Narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving arctic air mass extended from central Canada into Montana and the surrounding region, bringing temperatures well below normal for several days. Mainly in the northeast corner of Montana, these temperatures combined with breezy to gusty winds to create dangerously cold wind chills for at least two consecutive nights.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Navajo MT-5 DOT site (MTRED), temperatures in the teens and 20s below zero and breezy winds created intermittent periods of wind chills of -40 or worse from late night of the 21st into the early morning of the 23rd. The peak wind chill of -44 was recorded from 12:59 AM to 1:01 AM and again at 2:40 AM on the 22nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arctic air came over the Divide on the morning of the 20th with snow and wind resulting in low visibility at Marias Pass and Essex. The snow and wind impact spread westward on US Highway 2 reaching Bad Rock Canyon by early afternoon with wind gusts to 50 miles per hour and heavy snow. Total snow amounts of 6 to 9 inches were common. Snow drifts to 4 feet were reported at Hungry Horse with a few power outages between West Glacier and Essex.

A strong snow shower between 4:30 pm and 6:00 pm resulted in low visibility of less than 1/4 mile and a peak wind gust of 48 miles per hour at Glacier Park International Airport. Both road crews and the public reported very difficult travel conditions during peak snowfall intensity.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple sites across Sheridan County, temperatures from the teens to 30s below zero and breezy winds created intermittent periods of wind chills of -40 or worse beginning as early as the morning of the 5th and lasting as long as the early morning of the 7th. The peak wind chill of -55 was recorded from 1:16 AM to 1:32 AM on the 6th at the Comertown Turn-OFF MT-5 DOT site (MTCOM).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The leftovers of an atmospheric river moving over the Northern Rockies brought freezing rain and rain which contributed to cause very slick conditions. Roads were treacherous due to other factors including hardened snow on roadways, frozen culverts, sub-zero ground temperatures, and standing water on some roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads and sidewalks became a sheet of ice from the freezing rain and caused: dozens of accidents with some injuries, several people fell on the ice and several school delays and closures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black ice was reported by Montana Department of Transportation on US-12 between Lolo and Lolo Pass. Chains were required at Lolo Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cooler and wetter than average December led to some improvement to drought conditions at the beginning of January. However January turned out to be a warmer and drier than average month so extreme drought conditions continue to persist across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start January, all of Petroleum County improved to (D3-Extreme) drought conditions.  These conditions remained the same through the end of January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cooler and wetter than average December led to some improvement to drought conditions at the beginning of January. However January turned out to be a warmer and drier than average month so extreme drought conditions continue to persist across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start January, ongoing drought conditions in all of Central and Southern Valley County was in (D3-Extreme) drought. On the last week of January, the far northwestern corner of Central and Southern Valley County improved to (D2-Severe) drought, while the rest of Central and Southern Valley County remained in (D3-Extreme) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls cause severe driving conditions during the evening commute near Missoula and also near Garrison.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL Snowfall rates between 2 and 3 inches per hour were reported in Missoula. Severe driving conditions were hoisted by Montana Department of Transportation between Tarkio and Frenchtown from 16:47 to 18:26 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With strong low pressure toward the northeast, and strong high pressure toward the southwest, the main steering flow for atmospheric winds were directed through western and central Montana. The stronger winds from aloft easily made their way down over the higher elevations of the Little Rocky Mountains in southwest Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the BLM Zortman RAWS site (ALDM8), the first 58 mph wind gust was recorded at 4:32 AM, and the peak wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at 6:32 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The week-long arctic cold spell from the end of December 2021 spilled over into the first day of 2022, with continued well below zero temperatures and pockets of light winds, which created dangerously cold wind chill values for many northeast Montana locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Lindsay Divide DOT site (MTLND), temperatures in the teens and 20s below zero and breezy winds created intermittent periods of wind chills of -40 or worse in the early morning on January 1st. The peak wind chill of -42 was recorded at 2:08 AM.

The peak wind chill of -49 was recorded at 5:55 AM at the Scobey airport mesonet site (K9S2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system from the Canadian Rockies pulled suddenly colder air into all of northeast Montana and easily brought with it, stronger winds from aloft down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across multiple sites in Central and Southern Valley County, wind speeds of at least 40 mph began as early as mid afternoon and ended in the nighttime hours. The peak wind gusts of 58 mph were recorded at the King Coulee RAWS site both at 2:44 PM and 3:44 PM as well as the Saint Marie MARCO mesonet site (07MT) at 5:50 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The leftovers of an atmospheric river moving over the Northern Rockies brought freezing rain and rain which contributed to cause very slick conditions. Roads were treacherous due to other factors including hardened snow on roadways, frozen culverts, sub-zero ground temperatures, and standing water on some roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions were hoisted by 8 am on the 12th in Bull Lake, Troy to the Lincoln County Airport. One Facebook report mentioned that four wheel drive vehicles with studded tires would slide sideways due to the standing water on top of ice on the country roads in Trego.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The leftovers of an atmospheric river moving over the Northern Rockies brought freezing rain and rain which contributed to cause very slick conditions. Roads were treacherous due to other factors including hardened snow on roadways, frozen culverts, sub-zero ground temperatures, and standing water on some roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of wet snow and rain brought slick roads. A park ranger said that the conditions were more of a wet slush. A spotter in Coram reported that sidewalks were slick from freezing rain during the evening of the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The leftovers of an atmospheric river moving over the Northern Rockies brought freezing rain and rain which contributed to cause very slick conditions. Roads were treacherous due to other factors including hardened snow on roadways, frozen culverts, sub-zero ground temperatures, and standing water on some roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain developed during the late evening hours and caused slick roads across the western Flathead Valley. Ponding of water was common, especially on Farm to Market Road where a couple of cars slid off the road by 8 am on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The leftovers of an atmospheric river moving over the Northern Rockies brought freezing rain and rain which contributed to cause very slick conditions. Roads were treacherous due to other factors including hardened snow on roadways, frozen culverts, sub-zero ground temperatures, and standing water on some roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple people reported very slick conditions due to the freezing rain. One person on Facebook reported that Blue Slide Road in Thompson Falls was the worst ice they had ever experienced since 1982. Another mentioned that they were going 10 mph and lost control. The slick roads continued through the 12th as someone from Sanders County reported that the roads were a sheet of ice. Several schools were delayed and two jackknifed trucks were reported on I-90 west of Alberton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions continued into May. A large storm system moving through on May 12th and 13th gave some substantial wetting rains which would help improve drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start May, ongoing drought conditions in Sheridan County were at (D2-Severe) conditions for the entire county.  By the third week in May the eastern two thirds of Sheridan County improved to (D1-Moderate) drought, while the western third remained in (D2-Severe) drought.  At the end of May the eastern half of the county improved to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions while the western half improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.

By the third week of May the eastern two thirds of Eastern Roosevelt County had improved to (D1-Moderate) drought, while the western third of the county remained in (D2-Severe) conditions.  By the end of the month, the eastern quarter of Eastern Roosevelt County had improved to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions while the western three quarters of the county improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.

By the end of May, the far eastern portion of Richland County improved to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions, the far western portion remained in (D2-Severe) conditions while the middle portion, making up the majority of Richland County improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions continued into April. In the meantime the northern and western parts of our area saw only average to below average precipitation for April so drought conditions stayed the same in those areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level Low tracked through southern Montana.  An inverted trough on the northwest side of this feature caused significant snow to fall over Eastern Montana and North Dakota.  Strong winds from the pressure gradient of this storm caused widespread blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front on April 9th followed by a very cold airmass across the region on the 10th and 11th, were important components to this spring storm. Relatively cold air was already in place when a strong Pacific system arrived across the Billings Forecast Area. The combination of strong upper level ascent, deep easterly (upslope) winds and temperatures favoring dendritic snow growth led to intense snowfall rates on the night of the 11th and morning of the 12th. The upper level low deepened and tracked slowly as it moved from Wyoming into the Dakotas, and was responsible for the long-duration blizzard conditions that occurred across southeast Montana. Overall, the strength, duration and magnitude of cold (record cold temperatures) associated with this spring storm made it an event for the history books. ||The combination of heavy snowfall and strong winds caused significant blowing and drifting snow, especially across southeast Montana where winds were the strongest. Some livestock were lost due to the snow, cold and wind. Several school districts closed schools. In addition, road closures delayed travel across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 13 to 16 inches occurred in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system from Saskatchewan directed very strong winds through northeast Montana, while easily spreading from aloft all the way down to the surface.  Sustained winds of at least 40 mph with some gusts in excess of 60 mph were found in multiple locations throughout the time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speeds of at least 40 mph were common in the Little Rocky Mountains from the night of the 4th through late night on the 5th, and again from the morning through the midday on the 6th. The peak wind gusts of 75 mph were recorded between 10:40 AM and 10:50 AM, and again from 12:59 PM to 1:09 PM at the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold trough moved into the state for more sub-zero temperatures and wind chills. Warning criteria wind chill values (-40F) were more common with this surge than the previous one as a 1044 mb surface high moved southward from the Canadian Prairies. The cold air was followed by strong westerly flow, which brought another round of strong surface winds to the plains and portions of southwest MT. Temperatures remained below freezing along the Rocky for a portion of the wind event and brought another round of ground blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: K1OM recorded a -44F wind chill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front on April 9th followed by a very cold airmass across the region on the 10th and 11th, were important components to this spring storm. Relatively cold air was already in place when a strong Pacific system arrived across the Billings Forecast Area. The combination of strong upper level ascent, deep easterly (upslope) winds and temperatures favoring dendritic snow growth led to intense snowfall rates on the night of the 11th and morning of the 12th. The upper level low deepened and tracked slowly as it moved from Wyoming into the Dakotas, and was responsible for the long-duration blizzard conditions that occurred across southeast Montana. Overall, the strength, duration and magnitude of cold (record cold temperatures) associated with this spring storm made it an event for the history books. ||The combination of heavy snowfall and strong winds caused significant blowing and drifting snow, especially across southeast Montana where winds were the strongest. Some livestock were lost due to the snow, cold and wind. Several school districts closed schools. In addition, road closures delayed travel across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 to 24 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system from Saskatchewan directed very strong winds through northeast Montana, while easily spreading from aloft all the way down to the surface. EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple sites across Central and Southeast Phillips County, winds speeds of at least 40 mph were common from early morning to the early night time hours on the 5th. The peak wind gust of 75 mph was recorded at the Malta Airport (KM75) AWOS site at both 10:55 AM and 1:15 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year began with an Arctic Air mass over the region, powered by a 1028 mb surface high. Temperatures plunged below zero as northerly winds were just strong enough to produce dangerous wind chills down to 40 below zero along the Hi-Line. Milder westerly flow quickly replaced this air mass and brought strong gusty surface winds to much of north-central MT. Temperatures along the Rocky Mountain Front remained below freezing, resulting in widespread ground blizzard conditions over the East Glacier and Browning areas. The strong surface winds were mostly over the plains resulting from 50kt+ 700 mb westerly flow, but there was a brief period of strong southwesterly flow over the southwest that brought criteria magnitudes to Madison County. Another surge of Arctic air moved into the region again after the high winds subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at 17 N Joplin measured a 55 kt (63 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start April, ongoing drought conditions Wibaux County was at (D3-Extreme) drought in the northern quarter of the county, while the southern three quarters of the county remained in (D2-Severe) conditions. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold trough moved into the state for more sub-zero temperatures and wind chills. Warning criteria wind chill values (-40F) were more common with this surge than the previous one as a 1044 mb surface high moved southward from the Canadian Prairies. The cold air was followed by strong westerly flow, which brought another round of strong surface winds to the plains and portions of southwest MT. Temperatures remained below freezing along the Rocky for a portion of the wind event and brought another round of ground blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Cascade station measured a 51 kt (59 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the area at the very end of the month bringing significant snowfall to primarily higher elevations of southwest Montana. However, banding of snow also resulting in higher snowfall amounts across the Blackfeet Nation and adjacent areas of the Rocky Mountain Front Range. Windy conditions also prevailed with the strongest winds confined to primarily southwest Montana. This weather episode continued into early December and is also included in December's Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold trough moved into the state for more sub-zero temperatures and wind chills. Warning criteria wind chill values (-40F) were more common with this surge than the previous one as a 1044 mb surface high moved southward from the Canadian Prairies. The cold air was followed by strong westerly flow, which brought another round of strong surface winds to the plains and portions of southwest MT. Temperatures remained below freezing along the Rocky for a portion of the wind event and brought another round of ground blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an Arctic air mass pushed across the Continental Divide it brought a period of heavy snow and very cold wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very low visibilities along with several inches of snow could be seen at the Hungry Horse bridge on a Montana DOT camera around 8:30 pm. Throughout the day of the 8th, multiple reporting stations measured wind chills in the negative 20s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance within a northwesterly flow aloft brought gusty surface winds to the Rocky Mountain Front and adjoining plains. Most gusts were below warning criteria with the exception of one observation in eastern Pondera County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station 5 N Ledger measured a 50 kt (58 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drought conditions continued into May. A large storm system moving through on May 12th and 13th gave some substantial wetting rains which would help improve drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start May, ongoing drought conditions Wibaux County was at (D2-Severe) drought in the far northern part of the county, while the rest of the county were in (D1-Moderate) conditions.  On the third week of May, the northwestern half of the county improved to (D1-Moderate) conditions while the southeastern half improved to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions.  By the end of May, the northwest and southwest corners of Wibaux County remained in (D1-Moderate) drought, while the eastern three quarters of the county improved to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous shortwave trough and attendant cold front dove south into the Northern Rockies, delivering a shot of cold air and snowfall. Convective snow bands along and behind the front were fueled by strong cold air advection and low level lapse rates of 5  to 8C/km. H700 temperatures quickly dropped to the -10 to -15 C range, which played a role in northerly winds gusting in excess of 40 mph at times. The combination of falling/blowing snow brought brief visibility reductions down to a quarter mile or less to many locations. The snow became lighter and steadier following the frontal passage, but did produce localized heavy snowfall amounts, particularly on the southwest side of Bozeman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. Snow Squall observed at KCTB. Visibility fell to a quarter mile for 14 minutes and winds gusted as high as 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across Central and Southwest Montana, leading to strong, gusty winds, small hail, brief downpours, and an array of lightning. Dry and seasonally mild conditions returned thereafter, leading to an increase of westerly winds in excess 55 mph across the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains. Cooler temperatures returned during the latter half of the week, with temperatures 10 to 20 degrees below average and widespread precipitation. Lower elevations saw generally a tenth to half an inch of liquid precipitation, whereas higher elevations saw several inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7.5 inches fell. Some settling and melting of the snow (from a warm ground) has occurred since snow began falling Thursday evening.

Temperatures remained below freezing along the Rocky for a portion of the wind event and brought another round of ground blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated equipment at Badger Pass estimated an event total of 10 inches of new snow. Equipment at Mount Lockhart estimated an event total of 10 inches of new snow.  Equipment at Waldron estimated an event total of 8 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous shortwave trough and attendant cold front dove south into the Northern Rockies, delivering a shot of cold air and snowfall. Convective snow bands along and behind the front were fueled by strong cold air advection and low level lapse rates of 5  to 8C/km. H700 temperatures quickly dropped to the -10 to -15 C range, which played a role in northerly winds gusting in excess of 40 mph at times. The combination of falling/blowing snow brought brief visibility reductions down to a quarter mile or less to many locations. The snow became lighter and steadier following the frontal passage, but did produce localized heavy snowfall amounts, particularly on the southwest side of Bozeman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter at 5 E Gallatin Gateway measured 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level ridging persisted across the state on May 19 with mild temperatures and breezy 25 mph SW winds ahead of a cold front from the Pacific moving across the region late that Saturday. A combination of these winds, in junction of 20-25% RH prior to the Spring green-up led to the development of this wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire of 500 acres broke out near Anderson Road 7 NNE Vaughn in Cascade County due to an unattended burn barrel, requiring several agencies, including six fire departments, the Salvation Army, and local citizens 7.5 hours to contain. No injuries or damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A core of 50kt winds around 10,000 ft AGL followed a cold front through the area late April 8th into the 9th. The strong pressure gradient behind the front helped translate gusty winds to the surface from aloft along the Rocky Mountain Front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level low pressure system moved from southern Idaho to Lost Trail to Great Falls and brought a continuous swath of 6 to 12 inches of heavy snow and slick conditions approximately 110 miles to the north and west of the track. An arctic airmass entrenched across northwest and west-central Montana enhanced snowfall, particularly across the Mission Valley and southwest portions of Flathead Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT road crews reported an estimated 8 to 14 inches of heavy snow between Somers and Polson. It was snowing hard enough that they had a hard time keeping up with limited crew early in the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level low pressure system moved from southern Idaho to Lost Trail to Great Falls and brought a continuous swath of 6 to 12 inches of heavy snow and slick conditions approximately 110 miles to the north and west of the track. An arctic airmass entrenched across northwest and west-central Montana enhanced snowfall, particularly across the Mission Valley and southwest portions of Flathead Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across northern Powell County with snow amounts reported up to 12 inches 3 miles southwest of Ovando. Gusty easterly winds between 10 and 17 mph with gusts to 30 mph were reported at Garrison Junction which caused blowing snow and lower visibility. Other snow amounts include: 4 to 5 inches from Philipsburg to Anaconda and 2.8 inches at Montana Tech in Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper-level ridging persisted and was the dominated weather feature for several days. Record breaking high temperatures, near single digit RH, and strong winds gusts have led to a Red Flag Warning throughout the 4 days. Numerous fires sparked as a result of incapsulated erratic gusty winds associated with isolated dry thunderstorms. The breakdown of the upper-level ridge, onset of strong winds aloft, and the isolated dry thunderstorms was the trifecta that led to an extreme critical fire weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire by the name of Eagle Creek Fire started approximately 30 miles south of Havre in the Bears Paw Mountains. An estimated 7225 acres were burned and required the usage of 173 response personnel. Lions Campground and Sandy Creek Campground were both closed during the wildfire and only emergency and local traffic were allowed on Beaver Creek Road above Upper Bear Paw Lake. The wildfire was contained around the 21st. At the time of publication, the cause of the fire was still under investigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year began with an Arctic Air mass over the region, powered by a 1028 mb surface high. Temperatures plunged below zero as northerly winds were just strong enough to produce dangerous wind chills down to 40 below zero along the Hi-Line. Milder westerly flow quickly replaced this air mass and brought strong gusty surface winds to much of north-central MT. Temperatures along the Rocky Mountain Front remained below freezing, resulting in widespread ground blizzard conditions over the East Glacier and Browning areas. The strong surface winds were mostly over the plains resulting from 50kt+ 700 mb westerly flow, but there was a brief period of strong southwesterly flow over the southwest that brought criteria magnitudes to Madison County. Another surge of Arctic air moved into the region again after the high winds subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KHVR records a -47F wind chill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year began with an Arctic Air mass over the region, powered by a 1028 mb surface high. Temperatures plunged below zero as northerly winds were just strong enough to produce dangerous wind chills down to 40 below zero along the Hi-Line. Milder westerly flow quickly replaced this air mass and brought strong gusty surface winds to much of north-central MT. Temperatures along the Rocky Mountain Front remained below freezing, resulting in widespread ground blizzard conditions over the East Glacier and Browning areas. The strong surface winds were mostly over the plains resulting from 50kt+ 700 mb westerly flow, but there was a brief period of strong southwesterly flow over the southwest that brought criteria magnitudes to Madison County. Another surge of Arctic air moved into the region again after the high winds subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at Cascade measured a 58 kt (67 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave moved through the southwesterly flow aloft that's been parked over the area this week and set off another round of severe thunderstorms over the area this afternoon into the overnight hours.  Weather pattern finally changed after the end of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site near Dooley measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph.  This was denoted as an NDAWN site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet start to July in most areas continued to improve drought conditions and by the second week of July, the whole area had improved to (D1-Moderate) drought conditions or better, ending the drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the first week of July, drought conditions in the western third of Central and Southeast Phillips County were in (D2-Severe) conditions while the eastern two thirds of the county was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  During the second week of July all of Central and Southeast Phillips County improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level low pressure system moved from southern Idaho to Lost Trail to Great Falls and brought a continuous swath of 6 to 12 inches of heavy snow and slick conditions approximately 110 miles to the north and west of the track. An arctic airmass entrenched across northwest and west-central Montana enhanced snowfall, particularly across the Mission Valley and southwest portions of Flathead Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slick conditions with semi's spinning out led the MDT to issue severe driving conditions on Interstate 90 and also Brooks Street in Missoula. Corvallis schools were delayed. Snowfall totals ranged from 2.4 inches in Stevensville up to 7 inches in southern Missoula, Lolo and Hamilton. Three midday commercial flights to the Missoula International Airport were diverted to Spokane because of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level low pressure system moved from southern Idaho to Lost Trail to Great Falls and brought a continuous swath of 6 to 12 inches of heavy snow and slick conditions approximately 110 miles to the north and west of the track. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL at Lolo Pass recorded a snow water equivalent of 0.40 which could equate to around 6 inches while the Black Pine SNOTEL potentially received around 8 inches of snow. Highway 12 was snow-covered and using the swath of snow as a guide, the Rock Creek area could have received 4 or more inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved along the Canadian border throughout the day.  This feature dragged a cold front through Montana that set off multiple thunderstorms.  Some of the storms became severe and one generated a tornado that impacted the town of Glentana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down 5.5 miles southwest of Glentana at 5:47 PM MDT, moving to the northeast and directly impacting the town of Glentana at 5:53 MDT.  Several blue spruce trees were uprooted, several grain hopper bins and some garages and farm machine buildings were destroyed.  The roof from a house was blown off.  The Glentana Hall metal roof peeled off on the south side.  Multiple farm machinery equipment were damaged.  Some straight line wind damage including one dozen power poles snapped, was observed 0.5 miles south of the path of the tornado on South Glentana Road.  The tornado continued toward the northeast and lifted at 5:56 PM MDT, 2.4 miles northeast of Glentana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds developed across northwest Montana on April 11th as a surface low deepened across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 100 customers lost power in the Swan Lake area as a tree fell onto a powerline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds developed across northwest Montana on April 11th as a surface low deepened across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minor power outage was reported in Greenough Park. A large fir tree was downed in McCormick Park. A poorly maintained building experienced shingle damage off 14th Street and Russel Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across western Montana as a closed low moved into central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of convective snow showers impacted the Flathead Valley, where 4 to 8 inches of snow was observed. Icy roads led to school delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across western Montana as a closed low moved into central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Transportation webcams at Lolo and Lost Trail Passes indicate several hours of heavy snow and blowing snow occurred. Trained spotters reported 8 inches of snow in Sula. At least 14 inches of snow was reported in the Lost Trail Pass area. The Montana Department of Transportation declared severe driving conditions on US Highway 12 over Lolo Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the Alberta plains led to strong winds across Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter 7 SSE Ulm reported 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north/south line of thunderstorms that stretched across multiple counties produced outflow winds well ahead of the thunderstorms. This outflow was very strong and produced winds over 60 mph. These winds produced an area of blowing dust across Interstate 90 just west of Hardin. Visibility dropped to zero resulting in a multiple car pileup which resulted in several deaths and multiple injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 4:15 PM MDT, a thunderstorm outflow moved through the Hardin area. It produced a wind gust of 62 mph in the 4:35 PM MDT observations from the Big Horn County Airport. Along with the outflow winds, blowing dust reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile on the AWOS. This resulted in a 20 to 30 vehicle pile up along Interstate 90 around mile marker 493 reported at 4:30 PM MDT, very close to the airport. Prior to this, the Montana DOT RWIS station at Arrow Creek Hill, 25 miles west of Hardin, reported a peak wind gust of 48 mph about 15 minutes prior. This accident resulted in 6 deaths and multiple injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough sat to the west and an upper level ridge was to the east near the Montana and North Dakota border, placing northeast Montana under southwesterly flow aloft.  A shortwave riding up this upper level flow moved through and sent a line of thunderstorms through the area during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via Facebook, damage to a house from a lightning strike.  The west side of the house was burned and some siding was knocked off from the lightning strike.|Estimated damage is $1000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across western Montana as a closed low moved into central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and icy conditions led to numerous accidents along the I-90 and I-15 corridors in the Butte region. The Basin Creek SNOTEL in the Highland Mountains south of Butte, reported a 26 inch snow depth increase in 13 hours. A foot or more of snow was reported in Butte's Terra Verde Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds and snow squalls developed along and behind a powerful pacific cold front across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall produced snowfall rates of 3 inches per hour. Visibility of one quarter mile or less and frequent wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph was observed. 1.4 inches of wet snow was observed at the Missoula Weather Forecast Office between 8:00 pm and 830 pm MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds and snow squalls developed along and behind a powerful pacific cold front across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds damaged utility infrastructure and downed trees across the Flathead Valley. Farm to Market Road in Whitefish closed due to downed trees and power lines. Downed trees and power lines were also observed in Columbia Falls, where a spotter recorded a wind gust of 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds and snow squalls developed along and behind a powerful pacific cold front across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds knocked down trees in Thompson Falls; 500 customers lost power. In St. Regis, high winds downed a ponderosa pine tree, injuring and hospitalizing a local resident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds and snow squalls developed along and behind a powerful pacific cold front across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 67 mph was reported at the Missoula International Airport at 11:40 pm MST on April 4th. High winds and strong low level wind shear diverted two flights on approach to KMSO. Power outages were observed in Missoula, Frenchtown, and Hamilton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds and snow squalls developed along and behind a powerful pacific cold front across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 mph was observed on Mt. Sentinel outside of Missoula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds and snow squalls developed along and behind a powerful pacific cold front across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 67 mph was observed at Point Six northeast of Missoula. The Montana Department of Transportation reported down trees in southbound lane on Montana State Highway 83 at milepost 34. The Seeley Lake Ranger Station reported two live trees, 25 to 30 inches in diameter, were snapped about four feet off the ground. Downed trees were also reported in the Big Larch Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds and snow squalls developed along and behind a powerful pacific cold front across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph were observed above 6,000 feet. High winds downed trees in Essex and Hungry Horse leading to power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail up to the size of quarters.  At one point the hail was covering the entire ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mission Valley Power reported downed trees on Finley Point and in the Synder Hill and Addy Lane areas. Utility crews reported two broken power poles and multiple downed lines. Downed trees in Bigfork and Ferndale led to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper-level ridging persisted and was the dominated weather feature for several days. Record breaking high temperatures, near single digit RH, and strong winds gusts have led to a Red Flag Warning throughout the 4 days. Numerous fires sparked as a result of incapsulated erratic gusty winds associated with isolated dry thunderstorms. The breakdown of the upper-level ridge, onset of strong winds aloft, and the isolated dry thunderstorms was the trifecta that led to an extreme critical fire weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station GW0625 Joplin 17 N reported 53 knot wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme critical fire weather concerns on top of a quickly moving cold front bringing strong wind gusts in excess of 50 mph accelerated a grass fire in Pondera County along Highway 91, three miles north of Conrad, named the Pondera County Fire. The fire was estimated to have burned 2186 acres, destroying a family of 5's homestead and causing road closures. A collective response by the DNRC, several fire departments, Hutterite colonies within the vicinity, and local farmers and ranchers aided in the firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system brought a transition of cooler conditions with a period of showers and thunderstorms., specifically along northwest Montana near Glacier National Park. An upper-level low brought additional moisture with southwesterly flow aloft as the upper-level ridge moved eastward out of the CWA. Additionally, SPC issued a marginal risk for severe weather this afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTLOM Loma (MT DOT) reported a 52 knot wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year began with an Arctic Air mass over the region, powered by a 1028 mb surface high. Temperatures plunged below zero as northerly winds were just strong enough to produce dangerous wind chills down to 40 below zero along the Hi-Line. Milder westerly flow quickly replaced this air mass and brought strong gusty surface winds to much of north-central MT. Temperatures along the Rocky Mountain Front remained below freezing, resulting in widespread ground blizzard conditions over the East Glacier and Browning areas. The strong surface winds were mostly over the plains resulting from 50kt+ 700 mb westerly flow, but there was a brief period of strong southwesterly flow over the southwest that brought criteria magnitudes to Madison County. Another surge of Arctic air moved into the region again after the high winds subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Dellwo station measured a 59 kt (68 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An open wave moving up from the south initiated multiple hail producing storms, and at least one with estimated severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a report of 1.25 hail from a storm that traversed the southern portions of Flathead Lake. Two greenhouse businesses in Jette, MT reported nickel size hail, or 7/8. A greenhouse received significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow produced slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snowfall brought snow-covered roads to the Flathead and Mission Valleys. Snowfall amounts include: 6.8 inches east of Pablo, 6 inches Creston, 5.5 inches Glacier Park International, and 3.3 inches in Ronan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow produced slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell with total amounts up to 18 inches in Heron, 10 inches in Superior, 8 to 10 inches between Dixon and Trout Creek and 5.6 inches in Alberton. Severe driving conditions were hoisted during the early morning hours of Dec. 1 on I-90 from Lookout Pass to milepost 33. Schools in the region were either delayed or closed because of the snow. Black ice with snow showers caused slick conditions and several semi rollovers during the evening of Dec. 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow produced slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow fell in the  mountains. Total snow from the two day event included 12 inches at Lolo Pass and 9 inches at Lost Trail Pass. Chains were required at Lolo Pass during the day on Nov. 30.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN GLACIER, WESTERN TOOLE, AND CENTRAL PONDERA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow aloft increased over the region during this period. Favorable temperature profiles at ridgetops promoted mountain wave activity. These mountain waves resulted in three wind gusts that exceeded high wind criteria across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station kctb Cut Bank Airport measured a wind gust of 71 mph. Additionally, a co-op site measured a 59 mph gust nearby around the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave and associated cold front brought mixed precipitation and snow to the region. A second wave initiated snow bands on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall Winds gusting between 30 and 40 mph combined with heavy snow caused blowing snow with whiteout conditions and icy roads. Reports from Facebook stated that Homestake Pass was really bad and also at least 2 dozen people were in ditches along Interstate 90 between Missoula and Butte because of icy roads.

Strong upper-level ridging persisted and was the dominated weather feature for several days. Record breaking high temperatures, near single digit RH, and strong winds gusts have led to a Red Flag Warning throughout the 4 days. Numerous fires sparked as a result of incapsulated erratic gusty winds associated with isolated dry thunderstorms. The breakdown of the upper-level ridge, onset of strong winds aloft, and the isolated dry thunderstorms was the trifecta that led to an extreme critical fire weather event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ambient weather station NNW of Selma reported 51 knot wind gust.

Initially, the thought was that mountain snow and lower elevation rain would occur, but as the event got closer, it became evident that snow at lower elevation would occur, which prompted the expansion of Winter Storm Warnings to lower elevations.

CZ_NAME: GALLATIN AND MADISON COUNTY MOUNTAINS AND CENTENNIAL MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep longwave trough overspread the region from the 22nd through the 23rd, bringing ample Pacific moisture for an impactful winter storm. Initially, the thought was that mountain snow and lower elevation rain would occur, but as the event got closer, it became evident that snow at lower elevation would occur, which prompted the expansion of Winter Storm Warnings to lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Search and Rescue teams faced difficult terrain and harsh weather while attempting a backcountry rescue in snowy and icy conditions. Details...Gallatin County 911 received a call from the Garmin International Emergency Response Coordination Center (IERCC), who had received an SOS from a satellite communication device.  After communicating with the device owner, it was determined a hunter had broken their leg while navigating rough terrain near the Albino Lake Trail in Big Sky.|Gallatin County Sheriff Search and Rescue Big Sky Section, Valley Section, and a Big Sky Fire Department responded to assist with the call. SAR Big Sky and Valley ground teams deployed on foot to the patient with ropes and a one wheeled litter. Another SAR Big Sky team deployed from the Eldridge Trailhead on a four-wheel drive side by side. Teams faced difficult terrain and harsh weather while attempting the backcountry rescue in snowy and icy conditions. It took volunteers several hours to locate, assess, and evacuate the patient. Once ground teams arrived at the patient���s location, they stabilized the injury and created a plan to evacuate the hunter by one wheeled litter, and then onto the side by side for transport back to the trailhead. The hunter was then transported several miles down the trail to the Big Sky Fire Department Ambulance and ultimately transported to Bozeman Health for further evaluation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 16 and 24 inches of heavy snow fell in the mountains across west-central Montana. Chains were required for Lolo Pass during the event. Very high winds in excess of 50 and 60 mph were reported in the higher elevations behind the cold front on the morning of the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river of moisture brought heavy snowfall and slow travel to the region. A cold front brought high winds and power outages, followed by snow bands later in the day on the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 10 and 20 inches of heavy snow fell in the mountains while 8 inches fell at Seeley Lake. After the passage of the cold front, winds gusted as high as 83 mph on the peak of Point 6 Mountain, and to 53 mph in both Clinton and Bearmouth on the morning of the 5th. A tree was reported down on Highway 83 near mile-marker 21, north of Seeley Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly 20 medium size trees were down with debris along Interstate 90 near Lookout Pass. Several power outages were reported including in the Thompson Falls area, and between Alberton and Superior.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An electric company reported 2,300 members were without power because of the high winds. A weather station northeast of Hot Springs recorded a peak wind gust of 61 mph. Another company reported a power outage along Highway 35 north of Finley Point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather stations in the Whitefish Range reported up to 85 mph wind gusts while lower elevation stations had gusts near 50 mph. Due to the concern for hunters being in the backcountry, the danger for treefall was very high.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell between Swan Lake and Seeley Lake. While only 1.2 fell just southeast of Bonner, radar estimates would suggest that heavier amounts likely occurred towards Clinton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers brought several inches of snow to the region, including 3.1 inches in Kila and 2.1 inches in Marion. The slick roads contributed to two fatalities on US-2 west of Libby at 1423MST in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist, mid-level occluded front interacted with arctic air entrenched across western Montana and brought periods of heavy snow, gusty winds and sub-zero wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills fell as low as 16 below zero at Marias Pass, but in the higher elevations wind chill values were as low as 43 below in Glacier National Park. Strong wind gusts close to 50 mph caused tree damage and power outages in the northern Whitefish Lake area and also near Big Mountain. Mountain top easterly arctic winds were very strong over the Whitefish and Swan Ranges and also in Glacier. Garden Wall saw gusts to 72 mph while the top of Mount Aeneas at 7,120 feet, gusted as high as 73 mph during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The new year began with an Arctic Air mass over the region, powered by a 1028 mb surface high. Temperatures plunged below zero as northerly winds were just strong enough to produce dangerous wind chills down to 40 below zero along the Hi-Line. Milder westerly flow quickly replaced this air mass and brought strong gusty surface winds to much of north-central MT. Temperatures along the Rocky Mountain Front remained below freezing, resulting in widespread ground blizzard conditions over the East Glacier and Browning areas. The strong surface winds were mostly over the plains resulting from 50kt+ 700 mb westerly flow, but there was a brief period of strong southwesterly flow over the southwest that brought criteria magnitudes to Madison County. Another surge of Arctic air moved into the region again after the high winds subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chinook AWOS records a -39F wind chill.

Reports on social media reported slick roadways in the Flathead Valley. Other snow amounts include: 3.8 inches in Kila, 2.5 inches in Ronan and 1.7 inches in the City of Kalispell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong ridge of high pressure settled over the Northern Rockies. Daily record highs were set for multiple observation sites.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With high temperatures ranging from 95 to 104F over 5 days, this was the hottest period of the summer. Missoula set record high temperatures 4 straight days from July 29 through Aug 1, and remained 100F or warmer for high temperatures for 5 consecutive days.  This tied the longest streak of 100F days ever.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic power outages were reported around Drummond and Butte, MT as winds gusted from the mid 40s to the mid 50s mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river of moisture brought heavy snowfall and slow travel to the region. A cold front brought high winds and power outages, followed by snow bands later in the day on the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow band produced 1.5 inch per hour rates across the South Hills of Missoula where a total of 4 inches fell. Other location snowfall totals include 2.3 inches in Florence, and 1.6 inches in Hamilton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow brought low visibility and snow-covered roads. Multiple accidents were reported on both Highway 200 and I-90 east of East Missoula. Snow totals include: 11 inches Seeley Lake, 10 inches Bearmouth, 8 inches East Missoula and 7 inches in Potomac.
