Additionally, multiple homes sustained damage, especially roofs that needed to be replaced. One homeowner spent $80,000 US dollars to replace their roof. A patio umbrella in Evergreen, MT was shredded. The property damage estimate could be underdone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow produced slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific front, followed by an arctic front initiated very heavy snow rates between 1 to 2 inches per hour, gusty winds to 25 mph with blowing and drifting snow, and very low visibility along US-2 between Hungry Horse and Marias Pass. Heavy snow showers enhanced by the existing arctic air mass caused more accumulation during the later period through early Dec. 2. Total snowfall included: 30 inches Pinnacle, 29.9 inches Essex, 17 inches West Glacier, 16 inches Coram and over 12 inches at Marias Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow produced slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow brought black ice and slick conditions both evenings of Nov. 30 and Dec. 1. A weak arctic front from the north produced gusty north winds to 26 mph and falling temperatures, which in turn caused the wet roads to ice up during the late afternoon and evening of Dec. 1 in the Missoula Valley. Multiple vehicle accidents were reported via social media. Total snowfall includes: 4.7 inches four miles west of Missoula, 3.8 inches at the Missoula International Airport, and 1 inch east of Florence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level Pacific trough brought heavy wet snow to northwest Montana. The region experienced one of the warmest autumns on record leading up to the snow storm which caused a plethora of tree damage and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 5 and 7 inches of snow fell between Marion and Kila during the event. There were a few power outages reported in these areas due to tree damage from the heavy weight of the snow. The higher elevation SNOTEL stations picked up 6 to 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level Pacific trough brought heavy wet snow to northwest Montana. The region experienced one of the warmest autumns on record leading up to the snow storm which caused a plethora of tree damage and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 1600 people were without power in the Swan Lake region due to the heavy snow damage to trees and power lines. The weather observer at the Hungry Horse Dam reported 13 inches of snow from this event while there was a report of near 10 inches in Ferndale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level Pacific trough brought heavy wet snow to northwest Montana. The region experienced one of the warmest autumns on record leading up to the snow storm which caused a plethora of tree damage and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility company in the Flathead Valley reported that this storm caused the most number of individual outages in its history as countless branches and trees fell on the power infrastrucure. Almost 57 percent of the meters on their power lines experienced at least one outage during the event which took 6 days to restore power. More than one thousand customers were without power in the Mission Valley. The city of Kalispell Public Works reported that they had so many trees and branches down everywhere that it took 2 days to clean up the driving lanes. A semi pile-up occurred on Jette Hill, located northwest of Polson. Schools in Lakeside and Somers called off classes for the day. Snow amounts ranged from 4 inches in Polson to 7 inches in Whitefish, and to 10 inches just northwest of Kalispell. The Ferndale are saw 9 to 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August allowed drought to return to portions of northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start September, drought conditions in the northwest corner of Western Roosevelt County worsened to (D2-Severe) while the rest of the county remained in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the second week of September, all of Western Roosevelt County had worsened to (D2-Severe) drought.  By the last week of September, all but the eastern edge of Western Roosevelt County had worsened to (D3-Extreme) drought, while the eastern edge remained in (D2-Severe) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August allowed drought to return to portions of northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start September, all but the far southwestern corner of Northern Valley County worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions, while the far southwestern corner remained in (D1-Moderate) drought.  On the second week of September, all of Northern Valley County had worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions.  By the last week of September the southeastern three quarters of Northern Valley County had worsened to (D3-Extreme) drought while the northwestern quarter of the county remained in (D2-Severe) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August allowed drought to return to portions of northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the first week of September, drought conditions in the northeastern half of Central and Southern Valley was in (D2-Severe), while the southwestern third and the southeastern corner of the county were in (D1-Moderate) drought.  During the second week of September all of Central and Southern Valley County worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions.  On the last week of September the eastern three quarters of Central and Southern Valley County worsened to (D3-Extreme) drought, while the western quarter of the county remained in (D2-Severe) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August allowed drought to return to portions of northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start September, the far northeastern corner of Northern Phillips County was in (D2-Severe) conditions while the remainder of the county was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the second week of September all of Northern Phillips County had worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August and record heat in early September caused the drought to expand further into northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the second week of September, drought conditions worsened to (D2-Severe) along the western and southern edges of Eastern Roosevelt County, while the northeastern three quarters of the county was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  On the last week of September, the entire area of Eastern Roosevelt County worsened to (D2-Severe) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August and record heat in early September caused the drought to expand further into northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the second week of September, the northeastern half of Richland County worsened to (D2-Severe) drought conditions, while the southwestern half was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the third week of September all of Richland County had worsened to (D2-Severe) drought conditions.  On the last week of September an area of southwestern Richland County as well as the far northwestern corner had worsened to (D3-Extreme) drought, while the remainder of Richland County remained in (D2-Severe) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August and record heat in early September caused the drought to expand further into northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the second week of September, the northwestern quarter and the far northeastern corner of McCone County were in (D2-Severe) conditions, while the remainder of the county was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the third week of September, all but the far southern tip and far southwestern corner of McCone County had worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions, while these two small areas remained in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the last week of September the northern quarter and the southeastern quarter of McCone County had worsened to (D3-Extreme) drought, while the remaining half of McCone County in between these two areas was in (D2-Severe) conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August and record heat in early September caused the drought to expand further into northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the second week of September, drought conditions on the northern quarter of Garfield County worsened to (D2-Severe), with the north central quarter of Garfield County in (D1-Moderate) drought, the south central quarter in (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions and the southern quarter of the county in no drought classification at all.  On the third week of September the northern third of Garfield County worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions, the middle third worsened to (D1-Moderate) drought and the southern third worsened to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions.  During the last week of September the northern third of Garfield County remained in (D2-Severe) conditions while the southern two thirds worsened to (D1-Moderate) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August and record heat in early September caused the drought to expand further into northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the second week of September, all of Central and Southeast Phillips County worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August and record heat in early September caused the drought to expand further into northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the second week of September, the northwestern corner of Petroleum County worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions.  Meanwhile the northcentral area of Petroleum County was in (D1-Moderate) drought, the southwestern corner was (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions and the southeastern corner had no drought classification.  On the third week of September the northwestern corner of Petroleum County remained in (D2-Severe) conditions while the central third of the county worsened to (D1-Moderate) drought and the southern third of the county worsened to (D0-Abnormally Dry) conditions.  By the last week of September the northwestern corner of Petroleum County remained in (D2-Severe) conditions while the southern three quarters of Petroleum County worsened to (D1-Moderate) drought.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the second week of September, all of the Little Rocky Mountains worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August combined with the warmest September on record with little to no precipitation has caused the drought to become more widespread across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Starting on the last week of September and lasting through the end of the month, drought conditions in the southwestern quarter of Sheridan County worsened into (D2-Severe) drought conditions while the northeastern three quarters of the county were in (D1-Moderate) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August allowed drought to return to portions of northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start September, drought conditions in the western half of Daniels County worsened to (D2-Severe) drought conditions while the eastern half was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  On the second week of September all but the northeast corner of Daniels County worsened to (D2-Severe) drought while the northeast corner remained in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the end of September the southwestern three quarters of Daniels County worsened to (D3-Extreme) drought while the northern and eastern borders of the county were in (D2-Severe) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across Central and Southwest Montana, leading to strong, gusty winds, small hail, brief downpours, and an array of lightning. Dry and seasonally mild conditions returned thereafter, leading to an increase of westerly winds in excess 55 mph across the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains. Cooler temperatures returned during the latter half of the week, with temperatures 10 to 20 degrees below average and widespread precipitation. Lower elevations saw generally a tenth to half an inch of liquid precipitation, whereas higher elevations saw several inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTM 52 measured 60 mph wind gust 7 miles SE Hogeland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought periods of moderate to heavy snowfall and gusty northwest winds to 45 mph. Snowfall amounts were mainly in the 6 to 12 inch range. In addition, surface observations showed prolonged reductions in visibilities below 1 mile. U.S. Highway 212 and Interstate 90 were closed for a prolonged period of time. In addition, schools instituted virtual learning and the Montana Department of Transportation urged no unnecessary travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7 to 8 inches with wind gusts to 50 mph were reported across the area. Highway 212 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Artic air plunged southward behind a Canadian cold front, bringing dangerously cold wind chills to most of North-central and Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors in Beaverhead county reported wind chills of -40F or lower on Feb 22 and 23, including mesonet station mtbhp Big Hole Pass (mt dot). Temperature of -18f with a sustained wind of 17 mph at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous shortwave trough and attendant cold front dove south into the Northern Rockies, delivering a shot of cold air and snowfall. Convective snow bands along and behind the front were fueled by strong cold air advection and low level lapse rates of 5  to 8C/km. H700 temperatures quickly dropped to the -10 to -15 C range, which played a role in northerly winds gusting in excess of 40 mph at times. The combination of falling/blowing snow brought brief visibility reductions down to a quarter mile or less to many locations. The snow became lighter and steadier following the frontal passage, but did produce localized heavy snowfall amounts, particularly on the southwest side of Bozeman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. Snow squall observed at KGTF. Visibility dropped to a quarter mile for 12 minutes and winds gusts peaked at 48 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow brought slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific front, then arctic front passage initiated very heavy snow rates between 1 to 2 inches per hour, gusty winds to 25 mph with blowing and drifting snow, and very low visibility along US-2 between Hungry Horse and Marias Pass. Heavy snow showers enhanced by the existing arctic airmass caused more accumulation during the later period through early Dec. 2. Total snowfall included: 30 inches Pinnacle, 29.9 inches Essex, 17 inches West Glacier, 16 inches Coram and over 12 inches at Marias Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow brought slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow caused very tough driving conditions across the area. Total snowfall amounts include: 22 inches Bull Lake, 12 to 18 inches Libby and Eureka, 7 to 10 inches in both Yaak and Trout Creek. Snow bands brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to US-93 between Olney and Whitefish during the evening of Dec. 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Artic air plunged southward behind a Canadian cold front, bringing dangerously cold wind chills to most of North-central and Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two mesonets in the zone reported wind chills from -42F to -49F from Feb 22 to 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave trough brought strong westerly flow aloft and lee side troughing for another round of strong gusty winds along the Rocky Mountain Front and portions of the plains. H700 winds up to 50 kts were transferred to the surface by the surface pressure gradient resulting from the lee side trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Station 14 W Bynum Dellwo 65 mph (56 knots) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening shortwave trough passed over the Northern Rockies. The dynamics with this system were rather impressive, complete with 50 to 70 kt mid-level flow and a strong cold front with H700 temperatures in the -10 to -15C range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors measured gusts in excess of 58 mph on April 5, with the peak gust (72 mph) measured at mesonet station fblm8 Fort Belknap RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved across the Billings Forecast Area. At the same time, a moist, Pacific flow aloft moved over this airmass. This resulted in heavy snow across portions of south central and southeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 69 mph, this combined with the falling snow and snow already on the ground, resulted in visibilities down to a quarter mile at times.

In addition, schools instituted virtual learning and the Montana Department of Transportation urged no unnecessary travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DES reported very poor driving conditions and requested an upgrade to a blizzard warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow brought slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell with total amounts up to 18 inches in Heron, 10 inches in Superior, 8 to 10 inches between Dixon and Trout Creek and 5.6 inches in Alberton. Severe driving conditions were hoisted during the early morning hours of Dec. 1 on I-90 from Lookout Pass to milepost 33. Schools in the region were either delayed or closed because of the snow. Black ice with snow showers caused slick conditions and several semi rollovers during the evening of Dec. 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow brought slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow brought black ice and slick conditions both evenings of the 30th and Dec. 1. A weak arctic front from the north caused gusty north winds to 26 mph and falling temperatures which caused the wet roads to ice up during the late afternoon and evening of Dec. 1 in the Missoula Valley. Multiple vehicle accidents were reported via social media. Total snowfall includes: 4.7 inches four miles west of Missoula, 3.8 inches at the Missoula International Airport, and 1 inch east of Florence.

A weather spotter located seven miles south-southwest of Butte measured 2.5 inches of snow in 1.2 hours. They also recorded a total snowfall of 4.5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across Prairie County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 8.5 to 20.0 inches. The greatest amount of 20.0 inches was reported by a trained spotter at 14 NNE Ismay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado Low tracking northeast across the Great Plains brought areas of heavy snow to eastern Montana.  As the storm departed the area, blizzard conditions occurred as strong winds developed causing blowing snow and low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported blizzard and whiteout conditions on US Highway 2 between Brockton and Fort Kipp.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Artic air plunged southward behind a Canadian cold front, bringing dangerously cold wind chills to most of North-central and Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors observed wind chills between -40 and -44F on Feb 22 and Feb 23, including mesonet station mtnor Norris Hill (mt dot).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Artic air plunged southward behind a Canadian cold front, bringing dangerously cold wind chills to most of North-central and Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors observed wind chills between -42 and -45 F on Feb 22 and 23, including mesonet station ncrm8 Newlan Creek RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight regional pressure gradient funneled strong winds aloft down to the surface to easily blow around recent snowfall.  Wind speeds in excess of 35 mph and visibility reduced to a quarter mile or less were most prominent in the northeast corner of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT Highway 24 was closed between US Highway 2 and the Canadian border due to the weather.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought widespread light to moderate snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow showers brought 1 to 4 inches of total snow. The highest amounts were observed along eastern sections of Flathead Lake and the Flathead and Mission Valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level ridge flattened out across the region, upper level flow increased across the area. This upper level flow downsloped along the Rocky Mountain Front and raced across the plains, resulting in wind gusts over 60 mph across portions of the area, particularly along the Rocky Mountain Front and along the northern slopes of the Little Belt and Snowy Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station 8 miles east-southeast of Fort Benton (mtm02) measured wind gusts to 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought widespread light to moderate snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers brought 1 to 2 inches of snow along the Highway 200 corridor from Plains to the Idaho border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought widespread light to moderate snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lindbergh Lake received 7.2 inches of snow with other areas in the Swan Valley seeing 4 to 6 inches. Lesser amounts of 1 to 3 inches were observed along Highway 200 and in Seeley Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought widespread light to moderate snow across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Butte and Homestake Pass webcams showed periods of moderate snowfall and winter driving conditions. 2 inches of snow was measured in Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continue to persist through the month of December for northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start December, ongoing drought conditions in the southern three quarters of McCone County were in (D2-Severe) conditions, while the northern quarter of the county was in (D3-Extreme) drought. On the third week of December, the far southern border of McCone County improved to (D1-Moderate) drought, with the northern quarter of the county remaining in (D3-Extreme) drought and the area in between remaining in (D2-Severe) conditions.   These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough and an Arctic air mass became established over the region for the episode. The initial frontal boundary crossed the Canadian border shortly after midnight Feb 20, passed through central/north-central Montana on Feb 20, and cleared the southwest by the morning of Feb 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 7 inches snowfall at 511 AM. The report with the highest snow total was 18 inches, at the same location the next day at the same time.

The initial frontal boundary crossed the Canadian border shortly after midnight Feb 20, passed through central/north-central Montana on Feb 20, and cleared the southwest by the morning of Feb 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated equipment at Badger Pass estimated an event total of 13 inches of new snow. Equipment at Mount Lockhart estimated an event total of 10 inches of new snow.  Equipment at Waldron estimated an event total of 12 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough and an Arctic air mass became established over the region for the episode. The initial frontal boundary crossed the Canadian border shortly after midnight Feb 20, passed through central/north-central Montana on Feb 20, and cleared the southwest by the morning of Feb 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 6 inches snow accumulation as of 4 PM, 10 hours after the onset of the warning. The greatest snow total for the event in Gallatin county was 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure ridge was gradually flattened by disturbances from the Pacific Ocean as it spread over much of the western and central United States during this period, making high winds a concern.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KCTB Cut Bank Airport measured a 66 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous shortwave trough and attendant cold front dove south into the Northern Rockies, delivering a shot of cold air and snowfall. Convective snow bands along and behind the front were fueled by strong cold air advection and low level lapse rates of 5  to 8C/km. H700 temperatures quickly dropped to the -10 to -15 C range, which played a role in northerly winds gusting in excess of 40 mph at times. The combination of falling/blowing snow brought brief visibility reductions down to a quarter mile or less to many locations. The snow became lighter and steadier following the frontal passage, but did produce localized heavy snowfall amounts, particularly on the southwest side of Bozeman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. KLWT observed a snow squall. Visibility fell to a quarter mile for 12 minutes and peak wind gust was 28 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 31, a snowmobiler was killed in a very large avalanche north of Cooke City, Montana. The avalanche occurred near Daisy Pass on Crown Butte on a southeast facing slope at 9,800'. Two brothers (age 17 and 21) from Washington were snowmobiling uphill on adjacent slopes. The older brother was climbing a steeper slope and triggered the avalanche 100-200' below the top. He was carried 600 vertical feet and buried 5 feet deep. The buried rider was wearing an airbag pack that was not deployed. Both riders had shovels and probes. They were not wearing avalanche beacons. A nearby group of riders rode up to the slide within minutes after it happened, saw a buried snowmobile and began to search for the rider. One rider from that group went into Cooke City to alert Search and Rescue. The buried rider was located with a probe line an hour after the avalanche happened. He was unable to be revived with CPR and AED at the site. The avalanche appeared to be 2-4' deep, 500' wide, 600' vertical, and broke on weak snow near the bottom of the snowpack. For more information, please see www.mtavalanche.com.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Episode write-up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture collided with an arctic cold front in northwest Montana, bringing heavy snow across Lincoln and Flathead Counties. Snow, blowing snow, and dangerous wind chills impacted areas along the Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT webcams highlighted winter driving conditions from snowfall along the I-90 and Highway 200 corridors near the Idaho and Montana border. 3 to 4 inches of snow was reported in the Trout Creek and Noxon areas, where school was delayed 2 hours on December 19th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of snow and blowing snow impacted travel on US 93 between Eureka and Fortine. 4 to 6 inches of snowfall was widespread across valley areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft resulted resulted in gusty downslope winds in the afternoon Feb 28. The surface pressure gradient strengthened along the Rocky Mountain Front and central Montana, which helped translate the winds aloft down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KCTB Cut Bank Airport measured multiple gusts exceeding 58 mph, including a gust to 62 mph. The first gust meeting criteria was 11 minutes before the warning was valid.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of 5 inches of rain combined with snow-water equivalent melt of 2 to 5 inches resulted in a total water event of around 4 to 9 inches. This resulted in unprecedented flooding across the Absaroka/Beartooth Mountains and adjacent plains. This flooding was rarely or never been seen before across many area rivers and streams.||Many locations were evacuated across the Beartooths and Absarokas with multiple communities completely isolated. Several water rescues occurred throughout the area. At the Woodbine Campground on the Stillwater River, 68 people were rescued and transported by raft to safety. Many bridges and roads were completely washed out and several homes/cabins were washed into the Yellowstone and Stillwater Rivers. ||The following river levels were recorded:||Clarks Fork of the Yellowstone near Belfry crested at 12.93 feet, shattering the old record of 9.97 feet set on 6/9/1981.|Clarks Fork of the Yellowstone at Edgar crested at 13.06 feet, shattering the old record of 9.5 feet set on 6/1/2020. |Yellowstone River at Corwin Springs peaked at 13.88 feet, shattering the old record of 11.5 feet set on 6/14/1918.|Stillwater River near Absarokee peaked at 11.02 feet, shattering the old record of 7.17 feet set on 6/2/2020.|Yellowstone River at Billings peaked at 16.50 feet, breaking the old record of 15.00 feet set on 6/12/1997.||The following is a list of the counties and associated impacts from this historical flood event:||Carbon County|-Significant flooding occurred on Rock Creek through the town of Red Lodge. Evacuations were needed.|-A significant number of bridges and roads were lost, including the 19th and 9th street bridges in town.|-North Side bridge on East Side Road south of Red Lodge was washed out.|-A water main was compromised and all town water was shut off for a period of time.|-Red Lodge Northwestern Energy customers lost power for a time due to severe flooding compromising the service lines.||The following areas were evacuated:|Access via the North and South Entrances of East Side Road.|Areas east of Broadway and west of Rock Creek from 19th Street to 16th Street.|Park Avenue|South Kainu Avenue|Island at Rock Creek -Kainu||A shelter was set up at the Gruell Building at the Carbon County Fairgrounds.|Church of the Rockies also served as an evacuation shelter.|Areas west of US 212/Broadway Ave., between the intersections with 19th Street and south to Adams Ave on the east side of Adams Avenue.|Lovers Lane in Belfry.|Town of Fromberg, locations east of 3rd Street East.||The following road closures occurred: ||-Highway 212 between 14th St and West Fork Rd|-19th Street Bridge|-Areas east of Broadway from 19th to 14th|-Roads along Rock Creek|-Kainu lane|-Park Ave|-Meeteetsee Trail at Rock Creek|-East Rosebud Rd #2177 on the Beartooth Ranger District|-East River Street at 3rd Street due to flood issues (Fromberg).||***Estimated damages for Carbon County $17.6 million.||Park County|Significant flooding occurred along the Yellowstone and other smaller creeks throughout Park County, including the backside of the Absarokas in the Cooke City / Silver Gate areas. Extensive flooding throughout Park County washed out bridges and roads, and left communities and homes isolated. It also made drinking water unsafe in many areas. The town of Gardiner became isolated and was surrounded by water. A Do Not Drink order for water was issued for the area after a water main broke. Cooke City and Silver Gate also were isolated and surrounded by water. The communities were isolated from each other.||The following impacts occurred:|-Carbella Bridge was washed out.|-Highway 89 was closed from MM 13-15 in Yankee Jim Canyon due to a mudslide with rocks and water on roadway.|-The Yellowstone River crested Highway 89 at MM 51 and was closed.|-Gardiner to Mammoth closed due to rocks and water on roadway. Road washed out in many places.|-3 feet of water over the Joe Brown Fishing Access site.|-4 feet of water over the road at Silver Gate.|-3 bridges on Soda Butte Creek were washed out.|-Mul Heron Bridge was closed due to a downed tree and water on the bridge. The upper half of Cinnibar Bain was inaccessible.|-Cooke City had no fresh water.|-The Yellowstone Ranger District office in Livingston closed due to advancing flood waters.|-Many people were landlocked, including the communities of Gardiner, Cooke City, Silver Gate, the Cinnabar Basin, Tom Miner Basin, stretches of U.S. Highway 89 South, Mill Creek, and Old Yellowstone Trail.|-Mill Creek Bridge and Pine Creek Bridge were the only two operational bridges in Paradise Valley.||Livingston Areas that were Evacuated:||-Ninth Street Island|-Much of the east side of Livingston including B Street east and Lewis Street south.|-An emergency shelter was activated at Hope Lutheran Church at 2152 Graf Street in Bozeman.||Sweet Grass County|-Main Boulder Road south of Graham Creek Trailhead was closed. The area affected was south of McLeod on the Main Boulder Road.||Stillwater County|-Nye Road at the Stillwater Mine collapsed leaving limited access to the mine complex.|-Mine bridge to their secondary complex was severely damaged and impassable.|-Rescue operation occurred at the Woodbine Campground past the Sibanye-Stillwater mine in Nye involving 68 people who were stranded due to the Nye Road being washed out. They were transported by raft on the Stillwater River past the portion of the Nye Road that was washed out.|-Evacuation ordered for parts of Stillwater County near Nye/Stillwater Mine area via Code RED|-The Reed Point area flooded. However, county roads and bridges were able to divert most of the water away from town.|-A bridge washed out in the vicinity of Chicken Creek on West Rosebud Rd.|-Extensive flooding occurred around Columbus, Absarokee, Nye, and Fishtail.|-Many voluntary evacuations as well as rescues from homes occurred.|-Many roads flooded and were impassable.|-The bridge at Riddle's Cliff washed out.|-A cabin floated downstream near the Nye Bridge.||Yellowstone County|-The mainstem of the Yellowstone River experienced historic flooding.|-The Water Plant for the City of Billings was forced to shut down for a period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Episode Narrative for information regarding this multi-county historic flood event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of convective and upslope precipitation brought areal and river flooding June 11 through June 16. Rainfall of 2 to 6 inches was common for the lower elevations of Northwest Montana between June 11 and June 15. Mountain locations received up to 10 inches of moisture over the same period. River flooding continued well into late June for the Flathead River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flathead River at Columbia Falls reached minor flood stage on June 12 as Flathead Lake reached full pool, and continued at minor flood stage until June 26. Impacts included groundwater flooding agricultural fields along the Steel Bridge Road. Also water flooded recreational vehicle sites and surrounded some trailer homes just northwest of the Highway 35 bridge over the Flathead River. This event did prompt Flathead County to spin up an Emergency Operations Center with the Sheriff as the IC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August, record heat at the beginning of September and little to no precipitation has caused the drought to continue expanding in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the third week of September, drought in the northeast corner of Prairie County was in (D2-Severe) while the remainder of Prairie County was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the last week of September the northeast corner of Prairie County had worsened to (D3-Extreme) drought, the rest of the northeastern two thirds outside the northeast corner had worsened to (D2-Severe) drought while the southwestern third of Prairie County remained in (D1-Moderate) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of convective and upslope precipitation brought areal and river flooding June 11 through June 16. Rainfall of 2 to 6 inches was common for the lower elevations of Northwest Montana between June 11 and June 15. Mountain locations received up to 10 inches of moisture over the same period. River flooding continued well into late June for the Flathead River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several areas of flooding and debris flows occurred, mainly on June 15. Trumbull Creek west of Columbia Falls, flooded a dozen homes near Highway 2 with water on to the highway. Flooding also occurred on North Hilltop Road along Garnier Creek and Blankenship Road near Columbia Falls with water running across Highway 2 from nearby hillsides. Debris flows occurred on the west side of Hungry Horse Reservoir forcing the closure of West Side Road in the Graves Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although recent rains continue to cause some improvement, drought still remains across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start June, ongoing drought conditions in the southwestern corner and western border of Northern Valley County were in (D2-Severe) conditions, while the rest of Northern Valley County was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the third week of June, all of Northern Valley County had improved to (D1-Moderate) drought.  These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving through southern Saskatchewan caused strong winds across northeastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For multiple sites across Dawson County, intermittent wind speeds of at least 40 mph were common from the mid morning until the late evening hours. The peak wind gust of 67 mph was recorded at 11:15 AM at the BLM Glendive mesonet site (MTM28).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and a few weak thunderstorms brought strong gusty winds to western Montana. The winds brought down a few trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from Hamilton remarked on Facebook that they had downed trees. A driver on Highway 93 saw 3 different trees split and fall between Lolo and Florence. A tree fell on Lost Horse Road with a power line down. Four cottonwood trees were blown down between Lolo and Florence.

A large low pressure system closed off over southern Alberta bringing a heavy, upslope snow event across the higher elevations of Glacier Park region. Snow levels fell to 4500 feet but the heaviest snowfall was found above 6000 feet from early on the 14th through the morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three feet of new snow was observed at the Noisy Basin and Stahl Peak SNOTELs. Noisy Basin reported a snow water equivalent increase of 9.70 inches with 7.80 inches at Stahl Peak. The Glacier National Park plow crew reported 18 to 24 inches of new snow on the Going-to-the-Sun Road at 6250 feet elevation.  Slushy road conditions were even reported at Marias Pass on Highway 2. Whitefish Ski Resort had to cancel all activities because of the late season snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a day of record heat, a cold front moved in producing elevated showers and thunderstorms that produced some strong to severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported two sheds damaged.  One was leaned up against grain bins and the other had its roof torn off.  A fabric carport was also damaged.  Winds were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August, record heat at the beginning of September and little to no precipitation has caused the drought to continue expanding in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the third week of September, the northwestern two thirds of Dawson County worsened to (D2-Severe) drought conditions while the southeastern third was in (D1-Moderate) drought.  By the last week of September the central third of Dawson County worsened to (D3-Extreme) while the northern portion and southeastern third of Dawson County remained in (D2-Severe) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry August combined with the warmest September on record with little to no precipitation has caused the drought to become more widespread across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the last week of September, drought worsened to (D2-Severe) conditions for all of Wibaux County.  These conditions remained the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the Alberta plains led to strong winds across Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-op observer station FTBM8 Fort Benton reported 66 mph wind gust. Time of peak wind gust estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Matt Staff Road Fire started August 4th on a Red Flag Warning Day. The fire totaled 1,587 acres east of Helena.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Matt Staff Road Fire was a fire that started on a Red Flag Warning Day approximately 5 miles east of East Helena, MT.  The fire quickly spread east, given strong westerly winds the day the fire started, and ultimately burned to about 12 miles east of East Helena. The fire started near Spokane Creek Road, just off U.S. Highway 12 near state Highway 284. It originally burned grassland, then crested the ridge between East Helena and Canyon Ferry Reservoir and began to burn brush. Cause of the fire has officially been listed as Unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire initiated in the Beaverhead-Deerlodge National Forest in late August and persisted into late October. This fire affected both NWS Great Falls and NWS Missoula's areas of responsibility and will also be noted in September and October Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trail Ridge Fire began in extreme northwest Beaverhead County, approximately 22 miles northwest of Wisdom, near the Beaverhead/Ravalli County line. 18,138 acres burned across the two counties with the cause of the fire being officially listed as lightning. The fire persisted through September and into late October and will also be listed in Storm Data for those respective months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow brought sufficient instability and shear to the region. An approaching trough provided sufficient forcing for ascent, resulting in a few severe thunderstorms across western portions of North-central Montana. One supercell dominated, beginning near Lincoln, travelling northeastward, finally crossing the Canadian border at the Blaine/Hill County border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nws survey team determined damaging straight line winds with speeds of around 80 mph occurred across an area approx 1.7 miles long and 1.1 miles wide. Numerous wooden power poles snapped along i-15. Flattened grass and shrubs across the area. Pea size hail and water flooded local ditches and streams. Time based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 34 year old female struck by lightning, which also injured a 40 year old male and another male of unknown age. Individuals were driving a vehicle that had a kayak strapped to top of vehicle. Strap came loose and the group pulled over and got out to tighten the strap when they were struck. Female was flung six feet, struck head on rocks, and stopped breathing. CPR was performed before individual was air lifted to salt lake city where she remains in the icu. The 40 year old male suffered burns and other individual suffered minor injuries. Time of event is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moved into the Treasure State from the west. SPC placed several counties in a Marginal Risk for severe thunderstorms, including southern Judith Basin County, Meagher and Broadwater Counties, and northern Gallatin County with the remainder of the CWA under a general risk. Primary impact was heavy rain, but had possible lightning, small hail, and erratic winds associated with the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 2.15 inches of rainfall in the higher terrain of Cascade Co.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough hot on the heels of an upper ridge resulted in moist southwesterly flow across the region. A front provided a source of lift for thunderstorms on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station gw0799 Galata 12 NNE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure along the west coast spread deeper moisture increasing the chances for showers and thunderstorms across the region through Independence Day. These storms brought strong, gusty winds, severe hail, moderate to heavy rainfall, and lightning, causing notable damage to property, specifically crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures of water up to homes near winnie and sanders avenues. Significant flooding of  intersections with fast moving water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system and its associated cold front brought a round of strong winds to the Rocky Mountain Front, the Plains, and portions of Southwest Montana. Shower and thunderstorm development occurred along and ahead of the front and produced localized severe weather. A strong pressure gradient due to lee side troughing over the Canadian Prairies also contributed to strong non-convective wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harkness station measured a 50 kt (58 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system and its associated cold front brought a round of strong winds to the Rocky Mountain Front, the Plains, and portions of Southwest Montana. Shower and thunderstorm development occurred along and ahead of the front and produced localized severe weather. A strong pressure gradient due to lee side troughing over the Canadian Prairies also contributed to strong non-convective wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: K1IM measured a 52 kt (60 mph) thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level ridging persisted across the Desert Southwest and Southern Plains, extending north to over the Central and Northern CONUS plains. A subtle wave lifting northeast from the Great Basin toward the Northern Rockies provided widespread lift, combined with a steepening lapse rate and weak to moderate instability to support development of scattered thunderstorms across Southwest and Central Montana. Primary threat was damaging, gusty winds. SPC had much of the CWA under a Marginal Risk with a portion of the CWA under a Slight Risk for severe storms on the 14-15th, General Thunderstorms 16th, and a Marginal Risk on the 17th for the Southwest and eastern portions of Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTDEN (Denton) measured 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough in place across the western CONUS gained a negative tilt as a shortwave traversed through it. A mid level low spread across the region bringing snow, but was an overall disappointment in terms of overall QPF/total snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shower Falls Snotel estimates 20 inches of snow. Additionally, photos were sent in showing a semi truck slide off in Gallatin county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough hot on the heels of an upper ridge resulted in moist southwesterly flow across the region. Approaching trough created a weak surface low in the vicinity, which resulted in better surface moisture being advected in from the east. A front provided a source of lift for thunderstorms on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station DW4821 Chester MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific weather system and its associated cold front brought a round of strong winds to the Rocky Mountain Front, the Plains, and portions of Southwest Montana. Shower and thunderstorm development occurred along and ahead of the front and produced localized severe weather. A strong pressure gradient due to lee side troughing over the Canadian Prairies also contributed to strong non-convective wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ARC-WTRI Conrad station (6 NW Ledger) measured a 58 kt (67 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 6 a group of 4 men from Iowa were riding in Lionhead. They had ridden in the area for the last 12-15 winters. They all had a beacon, shovel and probe, and 3 of the 4 had airbag packs, including the victim. They all had taken avalanche awareness classes multiple times and read the GNFAC avalanche forecast that morning. The site of the avalanche is locally referred to as ���Ski Hill���, a common slope for snowmobilers to ride ever since a large avalanche cleared trees from the bottom of the path in March 2012. The party knew this was avalanche terrain and had seen the slope slide before.|Bradie (30 years old), the victim, was an expert rider who spent 3 winters in the area. No one witnessed the avalanche, but his brother believes he was sidehilling across the slope, which he had done earlier. Two of the 4 were on top of the slope and out of view. His brother rode up the hill from the bottom of Denny Creek when he saw avalanche debris and Bradie���s flipped sled. Further upslope he spotted orange fabric from a deflated airbag against a tree. He made his way over on sled and foot. It is undetermined if Bradie deployed the airbag or if the zipper opened and spilled the fabric out of the pack. The bright color signaled his location. The brother estimates 10-15 minutes passed from when the avalanche was triggered to when he saw it. It took him another 5-10 minutes to reach and uncover his brother. Bradie had his back pressed against a tree, head helmeted and slightly uphill, but buried under a foot of debris. He was not breathing, although his brother heard a few gasps after digging him out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gallatin National Forest Avalanche Center reports a snowmobiler was caught in a natural avalanche near the Lionhead Recreation area. Individual was carried into trees by the avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft resulted resulted in gusty downslope winds in the afternoon Feb 28. The surface pressure gradient strengthened along the Rocky Mountain Front and central Montana, which helped translate the winds aloft down to the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a summer storm shifted eastward, wrap around strong mid level flow was mixed down to the surface over the eastern plains, producing another area of strong winds. Additionally, Heavy rain fell over the Bearspaw Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough in place across the western CONUS gained a negative tilt as a shortwave traversed through it. A mid level low spread across the region bringing snow, but was an overall disappointment in terms of overall QPF/total snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure along the west coast spread deeper moisture increasing the chances for showers and thunderstorms across the region through Independence Day. These storms brought strong, gusty winds, severe hail, moderate to heavy rainfall, and lightning, causing notable damage to property, specifically crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding across downtown and Last Chance Gulch area. Severe businesses sustained damage, including Helena YWCA and the Lewis and Clark Library. 1 to 2 feet of water flowing up against business doors. Flood waters up to bumpers of vans. Moviegoers at the Helena Cinemark report the movie theater being evacuated, with water draining into the lobby area. Some vehicles were also initially stranded in the lower level of the Great Northern Parking Garage. Several streets in the Capitol area saw rapid flooding with a number of homeowners reporting basement flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance moved east across Central Montana producing thunderstorms late Wednesday afternoon. Some of these storms were severe, producing strong, gusty winds, some small hail, and lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level ridging persisted across the Desert Southwest and Southern Plains, extending north to over the Central and Northern CONUS plains. A subtle wave lifting northeast from the Great Basin toward the Northern Rockies provided widespread lift, combined with a steepening lapse rate and weak to moderate instability to support development of scattered thunderstorms across Southwest and Central Montana. Primary threat was damaging, gusty winds. SPC had much of the CWA under a Marginal Risk with a portion of the CWA under a Slight Risk for severe storms on the 14-15th, General Thunderstorms 16th, and a Marginal Risk on the 17th for the Southwest and eastern portions of Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station BRRM8 Brenner RAWS measured a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot afternoon temperatures and yet another round of afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms were expected over Southwest and portions of Central Montana. Max temperature was 94 at 2:44 PM, with RH at 23% as of 2:00 PM, and wind gusts just above 20 mph. Lightning activity, in union with the aforementioned variables, and heavy timber fuels in the Moors Mountain led to a wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Moors Mountain Fire, first reported on July 16th at approximately 3:45 PM near the Moors Mountains in Gates of the Mountain Wilderness, was a 98 acre wildfire caused by lightning. On July 24th, crews achieved 100% containment. More than 80 personnel worked out of spike camps in the Gates of the Mountain Wilderness, for multiple operational periods, and were supported by helicopter operations to supply the camps and to provide fire suppression. Crews utilized handlines which were tied into natural features, such as rock formations, to minimize the impact on the wilderness area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions were favorable at times for avalanches to occur across the higher terrain of Southwest Montana. A couple of the more significant avalanches have been included in Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two natural soft slab avalanches near Ernie Miller ridge. Avalanche warning in effect at the time. Observers noted many collapses and whumpfs in the snowpack. Looking toward Ernie Miller Ridge, observers noted an old crown in the main bowl just below the summit. It was on an ENE aspect, 200 or more feet wide and looked to be filled in with new snow but was still visible. Looking east from the top of the ridge, observers noted another crown on a west aspect that looked to be about 100 wide and deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the area at the very end November, continuing into early December, and brought significant snowfall to primarily higher elevations of southwest Montana. However, banding of snow also resulted in higher snowfall amounts across the Blackfeet Nation and adjacent areas of the Rocky Mountain Front Range. Windy conditions also prevailed with the strongest winds confined to primarily southwest Montana. This weather episode is also included in December's Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors measured wind chills of less than -40 F, including ASOS station KBZN which measured a minimum wind chill of -53 F. The high temperature for December 22nd at KBZN was only -24 F. Approximately 75 reports of broken water pipes were noted across the Gallatin Valley area due to the extreme cold.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start November, ongoing drought conditions in all but the far northwestern corner of Richland County was in (D2-Severe) conditions, while the far northwestern corner of the county remained in (D3-Extreme) drought. These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite November being colder than average and having above normal precipitation for the month, the drought picture over northeast Montana remains relatively unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start November, ongoing drought conditions in the northern tip of Garfield County was in (D3-Extreme) drought, the remainder of the northern third of Garfield County was in (D2-Severe) conditions while the southern two thirds were in (D1-Moderate) drought. These conditions would remain the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta Clipper system developed over southern Alberta and sped to the east over Saskatchewan.  A strong pressure gradient was generated by the low as it strengthened, causing strong and gusty winds over northeast Montana with numerous gusts over 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent wind speeds of at least 40 mph were common in Central and Southern Valley County from late morning to the night time hours. The peak wind gust was 81 mph at the Saint Marie Marco mesonet site (07MT) at 9:10 PM, with another high wind gust of 74 mph at the Saint Marie Marco mesonet site (07MT) at 12:50 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta Clipper system developed over southern Alberta and sped to the east over Saskatchewan.  A strong pressure gradient was generated by the low as it strengthened, causing strong and gusty winds over northeast Montana with numerous gusts over 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a camper was rolled over in the wind and torn to pieces.  This occurred at 7 SE Frenchman Reservoir.  Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level Low moved in from Idaho into southeast Montana bringing heavy snowfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across Sheridan County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 6.0 to 10.0 inches. The greatest amount of 10.0 inches was reported by public, via Facebook at 2 SE Coalridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level Low moved in from Idaho into southeast Montana bringing heavy snowfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across Richland County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 1.5 to 6.0 inches. The greatest amount of 6.0 inches was reported by public, via Facebook at 3 NW Lambert.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across Central and Southeast Phillips County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 7.1 to 9.0 inches. The greatest amount of 9.0 inches was reported by public, via Facebook at Saco.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across Garfield County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 6.0 to 12.0 inches. The greatest amount of 12.0 inches was reported by public, via Facebook at 4 SSE Sand Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak weather system passing through the area brought with it light snow as well as caused some areas of dense fog across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Big Sky Field Airport AWOS site (KS85), visibility of a quarter mile or less was recorded for about 4 hours on the morning of the 15th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent wind speeds of 40 mph were common in Dawson County during from the evening to nighttime hours.  The peak wind gust of 71 mph was recorded at 7:31 PM at the GW0525 Bloomfield mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and Pacific moisture combined with arctic air stalled over northwest Montana, led to multiple hours of heavy snow rates and difficult travel. Strong arctic winds brought wind chills down to 70 below zero along the Divide and dangerous wind chills across western Montana. A flash freeze and blowing snow were observed across west-central Montana when the arctic cold front pushed through overnight. As skies cleared, extreme cold to 50 below zero were observed along the Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell between 35 and 43 below zero. Butte dropped to 40 below zero. Discovery Ski Area closed due to extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and Pacific moisture combined with arctic air stalled over northwest Montana, led to multiple hours of heavy snow rates and difficult travel. Strong arctic winds brought wind chills down to 70 below zero along the Divide and dangerous wind chills across western Montana. A flash freeze and blowing snow were observed across west-central Montana when the arctic cold front pushed through overnight. As skies cleared, extreme cold to 50 below zero were observed along the Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, blowing and drifting snow, and dangerous wind chills impacted areas along US Highway 12 and Interstate 90, prompting MDT to declare severe driving conditions. Numerous slide offs occurred between Garrison Junction and Deer Lodge, where visibility below one quarter of a mile was observed by area spotters and mesonets. Wind chills dropped between 30 and 50 below as north-northeast winds reached 20 to 30 mph and temperatures dropped in the teens to low 20s below zero. MacDonald Pass reported a wind chill of 60 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and Pacific moisture combined with arctic air stalled over northwest Montana, led to multiple hours of heavy snow rates and difficult travel. Strong arctic winds brought wind chills down to 70 below zero along the Divide and dangerous wind chills across western Montana. A flash freeze and blowing snow were observed across west-central Montana when the arctic cold front pushed through overnight. As skies cleared, extreme cold to 50 below zero were observed along the Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadways experienced a flash freeze, and blowing and drifting snow as the arctic front arrived, which led to MDT declaring severe driving conditions. Numerous schools closed due to weather on December 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and Pacific moisture combined with arctic air stalled over northwest Montana, led to multiple hours of heavy snow rates and difficult travel. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures on December 22nd reached 49 below in Potomac, and 41 below in Seeley Lake and in Clearwater Junction. Extreme cold closed Snowbowl Ski Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual arctic air and cold ground temperatures set the stage for widespread freezing rain across western Montana as a trough of low pressure brought warm pacific air and precipitation into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT declared severe driving conditions on US Highway 2 as freezing rain lead to extremely slick conditions. Two tenths of an inch of freezing rain was reported in Essex. A downed tree caused 378 customers to lose power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual arctic air and cold ground temperatures set the stage for widespread freezing rain across western Montana as a trough of low pressure brought warm pacific air and precipitation into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain impacted the Interstate 90 and Highway 200 corridors, where up to a quarter inch of ice accretion was reported. MDT declared severe driving for Interstate 90 between Frenchtown and Lookout Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual arctic air and cold ground temperatures set the stage for widespread freezing rain across western Montana as a trough of low pressure brought warm pacific air and precipitation into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula International Airport briefly closed due to freezing rain during the morning hours of December 25th. A light glaze of ice was reported on area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual arctic air and cold ground temperatures set the stage for widespread freezing rain across western Montana as a trough of low pressure brought warm pacific air and precipitation into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice on roadways from freezing rain caused MDT to declare severe driving conditions on US 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual arctic air and cold ground temperatures set the stage for widespread freezing rain across western Montana as a trough of low pressure brought warm pacific air and precipitation into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain led to dangerous driving conditions and several slide offs on area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual arctic air and cold ground temperatures set the stage for widespread freezing rain across western Montana as a trough of low pressure brought warm pacific air and precipitation into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain led to extremely difficult travel along Interstate 90 and US Highway 12 from Bearmouth to Garrison Junction to MacDonald Pass. Numerous slide offs occurred. A glaze of ice also was reported along Highway 200.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual arctic air and cold ground temperatures set the stage for widespread freezing rain across western Montana as a trough of low pressure brought warm pacific air and precipitation into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions on Highway 93 from mile marker 56 to 75 and on Highway 35 from mile marker 0 to 18. KGPI closed from 14:12 to 23:50 MST on 12-25, due to freezing rain. Many reports of dangerous driving conditions and ice accumulations up to one quarter inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and Pacific moisture combined with arctic air stalled over northwest Montana, led to multiple hours of heavy snow rates and difficult travel. Strong arctic winds brought wind chills down to 70 below zero along the Divide and dangerous wind chills across western Montana. A flash freeze and blowing snow were observed across west-central Montana when the arctic cold front pushed through overnight. As skies cleared, extreme cold to 50 below zero were observed along the Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and east-northeast winds of 20-30 mph with gusts to 45 mph led to periods of whiteout conditions on US Highway 2 east of West Glacier. MDT and the Essex COOP reported 29 inches of snowfall. 14 inches of snow was measured in West Glacier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous moisture plume and arctic airmass brought several feet of snow to northwest Montana. Elsewhere, moderate snow brought slick travel along the Interstate 90 and US-93 corridors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow through Dec. 1 brought up to 10 inches of snow four miles north of Seeley Lake, and 6 inches in both Clinton and Condon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture collided with an arctic cold front in northwest Montana, bringing heavy snow across Lincoln and Flathead Counties. Snow, blowing snow, and dangerous wind chills impacted areas along the Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, along with blowing and drifting snow caused very tough driving conditions across the area, particularly from Essex to Marias Pass on US Highway 2. Wind chills fell to 30 to 35 below zero between Essex and Marias Pass and reached as low as 55 below in the mountains of Glacier National Park. Snow totals include: 15 inches in West Glacier and 15.9 inches in Essex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture collided with an arctic cold front in northwest Montana, bringing heavy snow across Lincoln and Flathead Counties. Snow, blowing snow, and dangerous wind chills impacted areas along the Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 and 4 inches of snow caused slick road conditions across the US-93 corridor. Snow and blowing snow was observed across northeast portions of the Flathead Valley, where wind chills reached 10 below zero as the arctic front passed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell between 20 and 41 below. Notable observations include: 41 below Olney, 39 below Yaak, and 31 below in Libby.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind chills of 40 to 55 below zero were observed. Snowslip Mountain in Glacier National Park recorded a wind chill of 70 below zero. Temperatures fell to 49 below at Marias Pass, 47 below in Polebridge, 36 below in Hungry Horse, and 34 below in West Glacier.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills reached 31 below in Thompson Falls and as low as 41 below in the mountains (Mullan Pass). Temperatures fell between 15 and 27 below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and Pacific moisture combined with arctic air stalled over northwest Montana, led to multiple hours of heavy snow rates and difficult travel. EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT declared severe driving conditions on Interstate 90 from Tarkio to Lookout Pass as snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour were observed. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph led to areas of blowing and drifting snow in the mountains. Between 8 to 14 inches of snow fell along the Highway 200 corridor, which led to cancellations of area schools on December 22.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT reported snowfall rates in excess of 1 inch per hour in Libby for 9 consecutive hours. Trained spotters in Libby reported 14 inches of snow. Snow, blowing and drifting snow led to whiteout conditions on US Highway 2 and 93.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and Pacific moisture combined with arctic air stalled over northwest Montana, led to multiple hours of heavy snow rates and difficult travel. Strong arctic winds brought wind chills down to 70 below zero along the Divide and dangerous wind chills across western Montana. A flash freeze and blowing snow were observed across west-central Montana when the arctic cold front pushed through overnight. As skies cleared, extreme cold to 50 below zero were observed along the Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snowfall, with rates up 2 inches per hour, was observed on December 20th. Snowfall accumulations of 5 to 12 inches was reported, with Bigfork receiving 14 inches. Arctic north-northeast winds of 30 to 50 mph led to widespread blowing and drifting snow on December 21st. Lake County officials declared emergency travel as area roadways became impassable. Flathead County closed Hodgson Road due to drifting snow. Numerous schools announced closures on December 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet and Pacific moisture combined with arctic air stalled over northwest Montana, led to multiple hours of heavy snow rates and difficult travel. Strong arctic winds brought wind chills down to 70 below zero along the Divide and dangerous wind chills across western Montana. A flash freeze and blowing snow were observed across west-central Montana when the arctic cold front pushed through overnight. As skies cleared, extreme cold to 50 below zero were observed along the Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped to 30 to 40 below zero across the Flathead Valley and to 30 below in the Mission Valley on the December 21st. Temperatures dropped to 34 below in Kalispell (7th coldest on record) and Hot Springs. Creston dropped to 35 below zero. Several thousand people lost power during the morning of December 21st across the Flathead Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills fell to 38 below zero in Missoula as sustained east winds of 15 to 25 mph were observed. Notable low temperatures include: 27 below Frenchtown, 26 below Stevensville, 24 below Hamilton, and 19 below Missoula.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT reported a flash freeze, blowing and drifting snow, as the arctic front arrived on December 21st. 12 inches of snow was reported at Lost Trail Pass, with at least 13 inches accumulating at Lolo Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lost Trail Ski Area closed on December 22 as wind chills dropped to 47 below zero. Widespread wind chills of 40 below or colder were observed across mountain sites. Temperatures dropped between 20 and 30 below, with the Skalkaho summit Snotel hitting 34 below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported along the southern Rocky Mountain Front as a strong weather disturbance moved across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter approximately 17 miles west-southwest of Bynum (close to the border of MT zones 307 and 308) reported blizzard conditions for 7 hours. A local volunteer fire department submitted photos from Stonewall Mountain of a communications tower being downed due to the weight of significant riming from snow/ice/wind. The downed tower had been there for 30 years and was the communications tower for multiple emergency response agencies.
